title: long filename support: app.manifest is not embedded in exe
author: spilkercompra
body:
Hi,

using a custom build from the current sources i encountered a long fllename error and noticed that the existing app.manifest is not embedded in the exe.

The project file
  src/GitTfs/GitTfs.csproj 
is missing a
  `<ApplicationManifest>app.manifest</ApplicationManifest>`
property.

Afterwards the long filename support is working.

title: Minor feature request - AppVeyor build to show checksums
author: snydergd
body:
Would there be an objection to having the appveyor build display the sha256sum of the resulting files and/or zip -- also possibly including a link to the AppVeyor result in the artifact?  I could create a pull request for it, but want to make sure this would be welcome first, and that there aren't objections or better options to consider.  I tried to find other issues/discussions on this topic but didn't find any.

## Use Case/Problem
I want to be able to know for certain that the software I'm downloading and running came from the source code I'm looking at in git.  This way I can review the code and be satisfied that there is nothing bad/sneaky in it (this is not a jab at the authors -- just in general, it's nice to minimize the amount of trust that is being relied on, especially when the choice could impact others).

AppVeyor nicely displays the commit hash it is building from, so I can know what it is building, but there's nothing I'm seeing to prove that the compiled code in the Github release is what was produced by AppVeyor.  If the AppVeyor output displayed a hash of what it produced, I think it completes the final link in the chain.

## Alternative Solutions
A couple alternative solutions that would work for my purpose would be:

- [SLSA attestation](https://slsa.dev/attestation-model)
- Code signing (I'm not sure that this would be able to cover additional files included in the artifact)

With those approaches, verification would be much simpler for me (I could just use a tool to check cryptographic signatures of the files, instead of having to rely on internet services for verification), but I think setup would be more difficult, and if AppVeyor didn't control the private key and logic for this type of signing, I'm not sure that it could be done.  Based on the limited responses to [this post](https://help.appveyor.com/discussions/suggestions/958-sign-deployments-with-appveyor-key), it seems doubtful that they do support this type of thing.

title: Missing folder after interrupted and resumed migration
author: fraluderin
body:
I'm using git-tfs to migrate a pretty large codebase from tfs to git.
I successfully ran a migration with the last three months history, but the team would like to keep the entire history (5+ years with around 25000 commits), so I tried to perform a full migration.
I used this command: 
`git tfs clone https://dev.azure.com/*** $/ -d "C:\Users\user\Desktop\migration" --username *** --password *** --branches=none --no-parallel`
The migration interrupted some times giving this error: "Unable to get artifacts from tool. HTTP code 503: Service unavailable". I read others issue and discovered that this could be because of network issue / Azure usage limits and that it's possible to try resuming the migration, so I did` git tfs fetch` and the migration seemed to resume from where it stopped. This happened 4 or 5 times.
A few minutes ago the migration completed, but a directory is missing in my root. Is there something I can check? Running again the migration would be an option, but it would take me another 4-5 days and I would not have the guarantee it would succeed.

git-tfs version is 0.34

Thank you in advance for your support and thanks to the devs who contributed to create this awesome tool.

author: jsmythsci
body:
Context: We are working through the process of migrating from TFVC to Git and are using git-tfs to export as much of the TFVC history as possible.

We encountered an issue where some of or Git clones did not include the latest commits that are present in TFVC. Upon reviewing the logs, the issue seems to stem from the fact that the top-level directory inside the team project starts with a `.`.

Here is the redacted debug output:
```
PS C:\Users\jasons\source\repos\tfs\CoreThingy> git tfs fetch --debug
Found matching Visual Studio version at C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\TeamExplorer
Command run:git tfs fetch --debug
Reading cached authors file (.git\git-tfs_authors)...
git-tfs version 0.34.0.0 (TFS client library 16.0.0.0 (MS)) (64-bit)
Commits visited count:1
Working with tfs remote: default => $/TeamAwesome/CoreThingy
No .gitignore file specified to use...
Establishing TFS credentials using user identity...
Identity-based VSS credentials created.
Fetching from TFS remote 'default'...
Commits visited count:1
Commits visited count:1
info: refs/remotes/tfs/default: Getting changesets from 100155 to -1 ...
Looking for changeset 99537 in git repository...
 => Commit 99537 not found!
Looking for all branches...
Looking for root changeset tree on $/TeamAwesome/.TeamAwesome-branch/Products/WickedApp/Trunk Looking for root changeset for branch:$/TeamAwesome/.TeamAwesome-branch/Products/WickedApp/Trunk
Looking to find branch '$/TeamAwesome/.TeamAwesome-branch/Products/WickedApp/Trunk' in all TFS branches...
Found parent branch : $/TeamAwesome/Products/WickedApp/Trunk
Merge: `Branch` C99311 `$/TeamAwesome/.TeamAwesome-branch/Products/WickedApp/Trunk` Source `Rename` C99296 `$/TeamAwesome/Products/WickedApp/Trunk`
Found C99296 on branch $/TeamAwesome/Products/WickedApp/Trunk
Begin process of creating branch for remote :$/TeamAwesome/.TeamAwesome-branch/Products/WickedApp/Trunk
Cleaning...
GitTfs.Core.GitTfsException: The name specified for the new git branch is not allowed. Choose another one!
   at GitTfs.Util.GitTfsCommandRunner.Run(GitTfsCommand command, IList`1 args) in C:\gittfs\src\GitTfs\Util\GitTfsCommandRunner.cs:line 36
   at GitTfs.GitTfs.Main(GitTfsCommand command, IList`1 unparsedArgs) in C:\gittfs\src\GitTfs\GitTfs.cs:line 83
```

title: git tfs clone not fetching all change history
author: ngarcia70
body:
version: GitTfs-0.34.0

We have initial TFS branching structure like below. This was created in 2019
+ Main
    + Development

In 2020, Dev1 was branched off Development.
In July 2021, Dev1 branch was renamed Dev2 and then Development branch was renamed Dev1.

That is the point where git tfs clone stops fetching change history.

git tfs clone will only fetch history up to July 2021 where the rename happened.

I have tried cloning Main branch with --branches=all, same result
I have also tried cloning Dev1 with --branches=none and --branches=all, same result

title: Error loading policy assemblies for VS2022
author: TimothyDJewell
body:
This issue is very similar to #1461, but since that user is no longer using git-tfs, I decided creating another issue was more helpful.
When trying to run the checkin commands, I get policy errors:
```
[ERROR] Policy: Internal error in Builds. Error loading the Builds policy (The policy assembly 'Microsoft.TeamFoundation.Build.Controls, Version=10.0.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=b03f5f7f11d50a3a' is not registered.). Installation instructions:
[ERROR] Policy: Internal error in Work Items. Error loading the Work Items policy (The policy assembly 'Microsoft.TeamFoundation.VersionControl.Controls, Version=10.0.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=b03f5f7f11d50a3a' is not registered.). Installation instructions:
Note: If the checkin policy fails because the assemblies failed to load, please run the file `enable_checkin_policies_support.bat` in the git-tfs directory and try again.
```

The full output I see in the debug log for the command (`git tfs checkin --no-merge --debug -w1234 some-branch`) is:
```
[Info] Found matching Visual Studio version at C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Professional
[Debug] Loading VS private registry for 'C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Professional\Common7\IDE\devenv.exe
[Debug] ApplicationExtensions:C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Professional\Common7\IDE\Extensions
[Debug] Configuration        :C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\VisualStudio\17.0
[Debug] LocalSettings        :C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\Microsoft\VisualStudio\17.0_5bde7576
[Debug] RoamingSettings      :C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\VisualStudio\17.0_5bde7576
[Debug] UserExtensions       :C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\Microsoft\VisualStudio\17.0_5bde7576\Extensions
[Debug] CommonExtensionsPath :C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Professional\Common7\IDE\Extensions
[Debug] CommonExtensionsPath :C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Professional\Common7\IDE\CommonExtensions
[Debug] Command run:git tfs checkin --no-merge --debug -w1234 some-branch
[Debug] Reading cached authors file (.git\git-tfs_authors)...
[Debug] git-tfs version 0.34.0.0 (TFS client library 16.0.0.0 (MS)) (32-bit)
[Debug] Commits visited count:2
[Debug] Commits visited count:1
[Info] Working with tfs remote: default => $/TfsPathHere
[Debug] Establishing TFS credentials using user identity...
[Debug] Identity-based VSS credentials created.
[Debug] Setting up a TFS workspace at .git\~w
[Debug] git command: Starting process: git diff-tree <Redacting args>
[Debug] <Redacting diff-tree regex debug>
[Info]  edit .git\~w\<file>
[Debug] get [C<Changeset>]$/TfsPathHere/<file>
[Warn] [ERROR] Policy: Internal error in Builds. Error loading the Builds policy (The policy assembly 'Microsoft.TeamFoundation.Build.Controls, Version=10.0.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=b03f5f7f11d50a3a' is not registered.). Installation instructions: 
[Warn] [ERROR] Policy: Internal error in Work Items. Error loading the Work Items policy (The policy assembly 'Microsoft.TeamFoundation.VersionControl.Controls, Version=10.0.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=b03f5f7f11d50a3a' is not registered.). Installation instructions: 
[Info] Note: If the checkin policy fails because the assemblies failed to load, please run the file `enable_checkin_policies_support.bat` in the git-tfs directory and try again.
[Debug] TFS Workspace 'git-tfs-<guid>;<Name>' was removed.
[Debug] GitTfs.Core.GitTfsException: No changes checked in.
   at GitTfs.Util.GitTfsCommandRunner.Run(GitTfsCommand command, IList`1 args) in C:\gittfs\src\GitTfs\Util\GitTfsCommandRunner.cs:line 32
   at GitTfs.GitTfs.Main(GitTfsCommand command, IList`1 unparsedArgs) in C:\gittfs\src\GitTfs\GitTfs.cs:line 83
   at GitTfs.GitTfs.Run(IList`1 args) in C:\gittfs\src\GitTfs\GitTfs.cs:line 48
   at GitTfs.Program.MainCore(String[] args) in C:\gittfs\src\GitTfs\Program.cs:line 39
   at GitTfs.Program.Main(String[] args) in C:\gittfs\src\GitTfs\Program.cs:line 27
[Error] No changes checked in.
[Warn] All the logs could be found in the log file: C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\git-tfs\git-tfs_log.txt
```

The output of the `info` command is:
```
$ git tfs info 
Found matching Visual Studio version at C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Professional

git version 2.41.0.windows.3

git-tfs version 0.34.0.0 (TFS client library 16.0.0.0 (MS)) (32-bit)
 C:\GitTfs\git-tfs.exe

Note: If you want to force git-tfs to use another version of the tfs client library,
set the environment variable `GIT_TFS_CLIENT` with the desired version (ie: '2015' for Visual Studio 2015,...)
Supported version: 2022, 2019, 2017, 2015

cloned from tfs path:$/TfsPathHere
git tfs clone https://<company>.visualstudio.com/ $/TfsPathHere RepoFolder --authors=Path\To\Authors.txt --resumable --no-parallel --branches=all

remote tfs id: 'default' https://<company>.visualstudio.com/ $/TfsPathHere
```

Other info:
* Only VS2022 (64-bit, Professional, version 17.11.5) is installed on the machine (Windows 11).
* I do sometimes run tf commands on a repository, but to do this I use the tf.exe built into VS, at C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Professional\Common7\IDE\CommonExtensions\Microsoft\TeamFoundation\Team Explorer\TF.exe
* I am able to run the checkin policies when using VS; my workaround is to shelve a commit, then unshelve/checkin in VS with tfvc.  It's very clunky.

The export of the registry keys from privateregistry.bin is:
```
Windows Registry Editor Version 5.00

[HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\VS_PrivateHive\Software\Microsoft\VisualStudio\17.0_5bde7576_Config\TeamFoundation\SourceControl\Checkin Policies]
"StanPolicy"="C:\\Program Files\\Microsoft Visual Studio\\2022\\Professional\\Team Tools\\Static Analysis Tools\\StanPolicy.dll"
"Microsoft.TeamFoundation.Build.Controls"="c:\\program files\\microsoft visual studio\\2022\\professional\\common7\\ide\\commonextensions\\microsoft\\teamfoundation\\team explorer\\Microsoft.TeamFoundation.Build.Controls.dll"
"Microsoft.TeamFoundation.VersionControl.Controls"="c:\\program files\\microsoft visual studio\\2022\\professional\\common7\\ide\\commonextensions\\microsoft\\teamfoundation\\team explorer\\Microsoft.TeamFoundation.VersionControl.Controls.dll"
```
These paths all exist as expected.

The first thing I did was run the bat file, and I can see from task manager that git-tfs is now running in 32-bit mode.
I read through the docs I could find (troubleshooting-checkin-policies.md was particularly helpful) as well as any relevant issues I could find.  #1499 was impressive and insightful, but I was not able to replicate the success or figure out how tfs is loading the assemblies.  I can see that `LoadFromVsFolder` will look for assemblies in C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Professional\Common7\IDE\CommonExtensions, which seems to be the path we want, but I don't see any evidence of it getting called.

The biggest question I have at this point is why tfs is looking for "Microsoft.TeamFoundation.Build.Controls, Version=10.0.0.0, ..." and yet is clearly also using client library 16.0.0.0.  I was concerned initially that it was using 16 instead of 17 (matching VS), but I verified that C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Professional\Common7\IDE\CommonExtensions\Microsoft\TeamFoundation\Team Explorer\Microsoft.TeamFoundation.Client.dll is indeed this assembly version.  The best theory I have at this point is the existence of a single file of a VS 2010 installation, which is C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio 10.0\Common7\IDE\PrivateAssemblies\Connectivity.xml.  The contents of the file appear to be related to various SQL data providers.

title: How do I whitelist branches on `git tfs clone`?
author: BagusTesa
body:
I'm using git-tfs 0.34, I have read [the documentation](https://github.com/git-tfs/git-tfs/blob/master/doc/commands/clone.md) for `git tfs clone`. However, I still can't wrap around my brain on how to whitelist branches I wanted to include in the clone. I have the following branches in TFS for Visual Studio 2022:

* `$/Projects/ProjectA/MyBranch`
* `$/Projects/ProjectA/MyBranch_DEV` (branched from the first branch above and will be merged to the first branch)
* `$/Projects/ProjectA/MyBranch_DEV_IMPL` (branched from the first branch above and merged to the first branch)

I ran the `git tfs clone http://my-tfs-server/tfs/confins $/Projects/ProjectA/MyBranch` in an attempt to migrate those three branches above into a single git repository with multiple branch.

However, due to how things work where I worked, `$/Projects/ProjectA/MyBranch` actually branched out from  `$/Projects/ProjectCore/BaseBranch` and we have many check-ins shared with other branches (not branched for or merged) from different projects. I have considered `--ignore-branches-regex`, unfortunately it proved to be cumbersome to list each of them in the regex. Even worse, I noticed that some branches with short path like `$/Base/Core` will be represented as `Core` to further complicate the regex.

I have tried `--branches=none` too, but I will lose both `_DEV` and `_DEV_IMPL` branches history. Is it possible to get only specific branches to maintain the history while ignoring the other branches?

If its not possible, how do I add the other three branches into existing git repository manually?
